note_data.title: Processor fundamentals
keyPoints: Processor Fundamentals is a core topic within the Theory Fundamentals section of Paper 1
The course requires understanding of computational thinking as a fundamental skill for producing solutions to problems
Skills such as abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking are used to study problems and design solutions
Computer architecture and hardware understanding is a key concept for the course
Communication is essential for data transfer between devices and components within computer systems
Programming paradigms provide different approaches to problem-solving and must be used appropriately

definitions.definition: A set of fundamental skills that help produce a solution to a problem, including abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking
A way of thinking about or approaching problems, with different programming styles suited to unique functions, tools and specific situations
The ability to transfer data from one device or component to another, including understanding of the rules and methods used in data transfer

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give characteristics or features. Use 'The CPU...' or 'The register...'
Give similarities and differences between two components

modelAnswer.question: Explain the role of the Program Counter (PC) and Memory Address Register (MAR) during the fetch phase of the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
modelAnswer.answer: The Program Counter stores the address of the next instruction to be executed. During the fetch phase, the contents of the PC are copied to the Memory Address Register. The MAR then stores the address of the memory location that contains the instruction to be fetched. The PC is then incremented to point to the next instruction.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: PC stores address of next instruction
Mark 2: PC contents copied to MAR
Mark 3: MAR stores address of instruction location
Mark 4: PC incremented to next instruction

commonMistakes: Not understanding that computational thinking involves multiple skills working together
Failing to recognize that programming paradigms must be used appropriately for specific situations
Not grasping that communication ranges from internal computer system data transfer to internet-based transfers
examinerTips: Ensure deep understanding of fundamental concepts rather than surface-level knowledge
Practice linking computational thinking skills to practical problem-solving scenarios
formulas: Addressable memory locations = 2^n (where n = number of address bus lines)
Data transfer rate = bus width × clock speed
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: CPU architecture diagram showing ALU, Control Unit, registers (PC, MAR, MDR, CIR, ACC), and the three buses (address, data, control) connecting to memory and I/O devices
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify which component controls the timing of operations in the processor
N/D 2022 P2 Q4(a) (3 marks) tested understanding of the fetch-decode-execute cycle, asking candidates to describe what happens during each stage
M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students had to explain the purpose of the program counter in processor operations
N/D 2023 P2 Q3(b) (4 marks) required explanation of how data moves between processor components during instruction execution
M/J 2021 P2 Q5(a) (2 marks) — candidates needed to distinguish between the roles of ALU and control unit

workedExample.question: Explain how the processor executes the instruction 'ADD 150' which adds the contents of memory location 150 to the accumulator. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: FETCH - Program counter contains address of next instruction
Step 2: Control unit sends address to memory via address bus to retrieve 'ADD 150'
Step 3: Instruction loaded into current instruction register via data bus
Step 4: DECODE - Control unit interprets 'ADD 150' as addition operation
Step 5: EXECUTE - Control unit sends address 150 to memory to fetch the data
Step 6: ALU adds the retrieved value to accumulator contents and stores result in accumulator
workedExample.answer: The processor follows the fetch-decode-execute cycle. During fetch, the control unit uses the program counter to locate the instruction 'ADD 150' in memory and loads it via the data bus into the current instruction register. During decode, the control unit interprets this as an addition operation requiring data from memory location 150. During execute, the control unit fetches the data from location 150, the ALU performs the addition with the accumulator contents, and the result is stored back in the accumulator.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: mentioning fetch-decode-execute cycle, program counter usage, control unit role in fetch, decode explanation, memory access for data, ALU performing addition

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the roles of ALU and control unit' — always specify that ALU performs calculations while control unit manages the process
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to mention the program counter increments after fetching' — remember PC automatically moves to next instruction address
Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses failed to distinguish between address bus and data bus usage' — clearly state address bus carries memory locations, data bus carries actual instructions/data
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw the Von Neumann architecture showing: CPU containing Control Unit, ALU, and registers (including Program Counter, Current Instruction Register, Memory Address Register, Memory Data Register, and Accumulator); Memory block; and three buses (Address Bus - unidirectional from CPU to memory, Data Bus - bidirectional, Control Bus - bidirectional). Arrows must show data flow direction and all components should be clearly labeled with their functions.
note_data.quickRecap: Processor Fundamentals is part of Paper 1 Theory Fundamentals, requiring understanding of computational thinking skills including abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking. Students must grasp how programming paradigms provide different problem-solving approaches and understand computer architecture concepts. The course develops transferable skills in computational thinking, problem-solving and programming that are valued by universities.
